input.prompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

input.initialPrompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about a .NET MAUI issue where the keyboard covers the text input at the bottom of the screen on Android. They mention that `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` was used in .NET 9 but doesn't work in .NET 10.

This is a known change in .NET MAUI / Android. In Android 11+ (API 30+), `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` was deprecated. .NET MAUI has been evolving its approach to handling keyboard/soft input.

Let me provide guidance based on what I know:

1. In .NET MAUI for .NET 10 (and even starting with some .NET 9 previews), the recommended approach shifted away from `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` because Android itself deprecated `SOFT_INPUT_ADJUST_RESIZE` in API 30.


2. The modern approach on Android uses `WindowInsets` APIs with edge-to-edge display support to properly handle keyboard insets. In .NET MAUI, `KeyboardAutoManagerScroll` was introduced in .NET 8 to automatically adjust views when the keyboard appears, replacing the older `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` pattern that no longer works reliably on newer Android versions.

3. For .NET 10 specifically, the challenge is that edge-to-edge mode is now the default on Android 15 and later, which fundamentally changes how the traditional `adjustResize` approach functions. The solution involves setting `android:windowSoftInputMode="adjustResize"` in the manifest while also properly handling window insets to account for the edge-to-edge layout behavior.

For MAUI apps, I'd recommend relying on the built-in `KeyboardAutoManagerScroll` feature that's been available since .NET 8, which should handle most keyboard scenarios automatically. If that doesn't fully solve the problem, the fallback is to manually manage insets in platform-specific code to ensure the IME doesn't overlap with input fields.

For a chat screen specifically, wrapping the content in a `ScrollView` with proper inset handling would be the most practical approach.
